title: Weft ikat: resist dyeing for woven pattern
subtitle: Binding weft yarn before dyeing to create resist-dyed patterned weft that produces blurred geometric motifs when woven
excerpt: Ikat is a dyeing technique in which sections of the weft yarn are bound tightly before dyeing to resist dye penetration. When the resist is removed and the weft woven, the dyed and undyed sections align to create a blurred, feathered pattern. This entry covers calculating pattern placement, binding the resist, dyeing, and aligning the weft during weaving.
sourceNotes: Weft ikat resist binding and dyeing method synthesised from general ikat technique references. Ikat is produced across Indonesia, Central Asia, and Latin America; the binding-resist approach described here is applicable to both acid dyes and fibre-reactive dyes on appropriate fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dyeing technique in which sections of warp or weft yarn are tightly bound before dyeing to resist dye penetration. The characteristic blurred pattern appears when the bound yarn is woven.

A material applied to or wrapped around yarn to prevent dye from penetrating. In weft ikat, the resist is tight plastic tape or waxed thread wrapped around the sections of weft that should remain undyed.

The careful positioning of each ikat weft pick so the dyed sections land in the correct position across the warp, producing an intelligible pattern. Misalignment is the main source of error in weft ikat.

The soft, blurred edge of an ikat pattern motif, caused by slight dye penetration at the resist boundary. Feathering is characteristic of ikat and is a deliberate quality of the technique, not a defect.

attrs.title: Dye safety
attrs.body: Wear nitrile gloves and work in a ventilated space when mixing or using acid dyes. Keep dye equipment separate from food use.

content.text: Step 1: Calculate and wind the ikat weft

Measure the planned weaving width at the sett you will use. Mark a guide board or table at that exact width. Wind the weft yarn back and forth across the guide distance until you have enough for the full piece, keeping all lengths at exactly the same tension and precisely the same width. Bundle the wound weft loosely but keep the alignment intact: the guide marks on the weft must correspond exactly to the edges of the warp.
Step 2: Plan and bind the pattern

Draw the pattern onto paper, scaled to the weft width. Transfer the pattern positions onto the wound weft bundle by measuring from the edge marks. Wrap each section to remain undyed tightly with plastic resist tape or waxed thread: the binding must be tight enough to prevent dye from wicking under. Use at least 4-5 layers of tape over each resist area. Bind all resist sections on the full weft bundle at once, checking that the positions align across all weft lengths.

Dye the bound weft bundle using an acid dye for wool or fibre-reactive dye for cotton, following the dye manufacturer's instructions. Keep the weft bundle as a single unit during dyeing so dye penetration is even across all lengths simultaneously. After dyeing and rinsing, remove the resist tape or thread while the weft is still damp. The undyed sections will show the resist pattern clearly. Allow the weft to dry completely before weaving.

Mark the edge registration points on the weft with a contrasting thread before winding onto the shuttle. As each pick is placed in the shed, align the registration mark with the selvedge warp end on the same side for every pick. Do not rush: poor alignment produces a blurred or distorted pattern. Beat gently to allow the weft to settle into the correct position. Check the pattern building correctly every 10 picks by looking straight down onto the cloth surface.

items.problem: The pattern does not appear as expected: the motifs are misaligned or broken
items.cause: The weft lengths were not all wound to exactly the same width, or the resist positions varied between lengths
items.fix: Use a firm guide board with pegs at exactly the warp width when winding the weft. Check resist positions on every fifth length against the pattern template before binding.

items.problem: Dye has penetrated under the resist into the pattern areas
items.cause: The resist binding was not tight enough or the tape was too narrow
items.fix: Use at least 5 layers of tape and wrap tightly enough to compress the yarn slightly. Waxed thread wrapped densely also works if tape is unavailable.

items.problem: The pattern looks sharp-edged rather than having the characteristic soft feather
items.cause: The resist boundaries were too precisely placed and the dye did not penetrate at all at the edges
items.fix: Leave the outermost layer of binding slightly looser on each side of the resist zone to allow very slight dye migration. True feathering requires some dye contact at the resist edge.
